concepts.title: Injectivity and Surjectivity
concepts.description: A function is injective if it maps distinct elements of the domain to distinct elements of the co-domain. A function is surjective if every element of the co-domain is the image of at least one element of the domain.

concepts.title: Types of Functions
concepts.description: A function can be one-one (injective), many-one, or none (neither injective nor surjective).

concepts.title: Composition of Functions
concepts.description: The composition of functions is associative, and the composition of a function with its inverse is the identity function.

concepts.title: Domain and Co-Domain
concepts.description: Use the mnemonic 'Dad's Old Car' to remember that the domain is the set of possible inputs (car) and the co-domain is the set of possible outputs (old car).

concepts.title: Function vs. Relation
concepts.description: A relation is a subset of the Cartesian product, whereas a function is a relation that satisfies the condition that each element of the domain maps to exactly one element of the co-domain. Students often confuse the two and incorrectly state that a relation is a function.

concepts.title: Key Properties of Functions
concepts.description: Recall that a function can be one-one, onto, bijective, or none. Also, remember that a function can be composed with other functions, and the composition of a function with its inverse is the identity function.

concepts.title: Range vs. Co-Domain
concepts.description: The range of a function is the set of all possible outputs, whereas the co-domain is the set of all possible outputs. Students often confuse the two and incorrectly use them interchangeably.

concepts.title: Inverse Functions
concepts.description: Two functions are inverses of each other if the composition of one function with the other is the identity function.
